elements.sent-2411-001: Generalist vision language models (VLMs) have made significant strides in computer vision, but they fall short in specialized fields like healthcare, where expert knowledge is essential.
elements.sent-2411-002: In traditional computer vision tasks, creative or approximate answers may be acceptable, but in healthcare, precision is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current large multimodal models like Gemini and GPT-4o are insufficient for medical tasks due to their reliance on memorized internet knowledge rather than the nuanced expertise required in healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-004: Meanwhile, existing medical VLMs (e.g. Med-Gemini) often lack expert consultation as part of their design, and many rely on outdated, static datasets that were not created with modern, large deep learning models in mind.
elements.sent-2411-005: VLMs are usually trained in three stages: vision pre-training, vision-language pre-training, and instruction fine-tuning (IFT).
elements.sent-2411-006: IFT has been typically applied using a mixture of generic and healthcare data.
elements.sent-2411-007: In contrast, we propose that for medical VLMs, a fourth stage of specialized IFT is necessary, which focuses on medical data and includes information from domain expert models.
elements.sent-2411-008: Domain expert models developed for medical use are crucial because they are specifically trained for certain clinical tasks, e.g. to detect tumors and classify abnormalities through segmentation and classification, which learn fine-grained features of medical data$-$features that are often too intricate for a VLM to capture effectively especially in radiology.
elements.sent-2411-009: This paper introduces a new framework, VILA-M3, for medical VLMs that utilizes domain knowledge via expert models.
elements.sent-2411-010: Domain expert models developed for medical use were trained to detect tumors and classify abnormalities (segmentation, classification), which learn fine-grained features of the data compared to coarse features that a VLM can typically process.
elements.sent-2411-011: We argue that generic VLM architectures alone are not viable for real-world clinical applications and on-demand usage of domain-specialized expert model knowledge is critical for advancing AI in healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-012: Through our experiments, we show an improved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with an average improvement of $\sim$9\% over the prior SOTA model Med-Gemini and $\sim$6\% over models trained on the specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our approach emphasizes the importance of domain expertise in creating precise, reliable VLMs for medical applications.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figures/vila_arch_radar_plot.pdf} \caption{Left: Comparison of VILA-M3 with SOTA benchmarks such as Med-Gemini and task-specific SOTA models. VILA-M3-40B performance is shown in comparison. It can be observed that VILA-M3 provides a generalizable better performance for all datasets. Right: VILA-M3 architecture overview, the model aligns visual features using a projection layer with textual user prompts and model cards describing available ``expert models''.} \label{fig:radar_arch_plot}
elements.sent-2411-014: Large language models (LLMs) are at the forefront of AI.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent works~\cite{li2024llava,lin2024vila have enabled LLMs to support visual inputs to expand on their use for many vision applications by transforming them into vision language models (VLM).}
elements.sent-2411-016: While foundational VLMs developed for computer vision applications have achieved remarkable success in various general-purpose tasks, they struggle to meet the necessary precision required for medical tasks~\cite{moor2023med, tiu2022expert, bhayana2023performance .}
elements.sent-2411-017: VLMs trained on general tasks often lack specialized medical domain knowledge to interpret radiological images correctly \cite{hayden2024performance, bhayana2023performance, nisar2024d .}
elements.sent-2411-018: VLMs without specific medical training also often miss the subtle visual details that are critical for medical diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, a recent study on breast imaging \cite{cozzi2024bi shows that generic models like GPT-4o (OpenAI) are not suitable for medical use cases.}
elements.sent-2411-020: At the same time, there are healthcare-specific large VLMs such as Med-Gemini ($\sim$1.5 trillion parameters) \cite{yang2024advancing , which are designed without consideration of expert model information and are likely to neglect finer visual features.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Current VLMs are well-adapted to connect coarse or dominating visual features with language.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, they often fail to recognize finer visual details (Fig. \ref{fig:qual_expert_seg ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: There are multiple reasons for these deficits.
elements.sent-2411-024: A major reason is the limited availability of comprehensive and clinically relevant public datasets for medical vision language tasks.
elements.sent-2411-025: Most public datasets in medical imaging were released to address specific narrow AI tasks like classification, regression, and segmentation~\cite{li2023systematic .}
elements.sent-2411-026: At the same time, healthcare datasets incorporating natural language are limited to specific tasks such as visual question answering (VQA) and report generation, which do not cover the breadth of clinically relevant tasks.
elements.sent-2411-027: Over the years, medical imaging has shown successful applications of specialized narrow AIs that solve specific medical imaging tasks, such as semantic segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-028: These ``expert models'' have already been incorporated into clinical workflows, such as pre-operative planning, patient triage, and more.
elements.sent-2411-029: Many have received regulatory approval (e.g., FDA) and, therefore, incorporating the ``knowledge'' of existing expert models into the decision-making and generation process of VLMs, specifically in healthcare, should markedly improve the overall performance on clinically relevant tasks.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we propose a new VLM framework, VILA-M3, that addresses the unique challenges faced by general-purpose VLMs when applied to the medical domain by incorporating the domain-expert knowledge of existing segmentation and classification models.
elements.sent-2411-031: We evaluate our approach on several medical imaging benchmarks and show significant improvements over the state of the art (SOTA), see Fig.~\ref{fig:radar_arch_plot .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Next, we summarize the key challenges that VILA-M3 aims to address.
elements.sent-2411-033: Limitations of Existing Medical Datasets: The majority of publicly available medical datasets used for model training and evaluation are static and not tailored for training for large VLMs \cite{irvin2019chexpert, wang2017chestxray, he2020pathvqa, liu2021slake, lau2018dataset, ghaffari2019automated, baemimic .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The datasets were originally designed for earlier machine learning approaches, and they do not reflect the complexities required for modern deep learning models such as VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-035: This need has sparked the generation of newer datasets such as \cite{xie2024medtrinity, chen2024detecting, hu2024omnimedvqa .}
elements.sent-2411-036: In medicine, the constant need for expert validation and the evolving nature of healthcare data make static datasets insufficient for accurate model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work, we outline the need for more dynamic and adaptive approaches and show that on-demand usage of domain expert models can lead to improved model performance.
elements.sent-2411-038: The dataset limitation is one of the primary reasons we require and utilize expert model information in our framework.
elements.sent-2411-039: While large models like GPT-4o have been suggested as potential tools for evaluating model performance in medicine, their reliance on memorized information makes them unreliable for precision-critical tasks \cite{hayden2024performance, bhayana2023performance .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth]{figures/VILA-M3_overview_v2.pdf} \caption{VILA-M3 possesses the capability to support a diverse range of tasks, including visual question answering, classification, and report generation. Segmentation tasks are performed by suitable ``expert models'', such as the BraTS brain tumor segmentation model for multimodal MRI. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-040: Combining General Vision-Language Data with Healthcare Data: Healthcare-specific datasets, while valuable, are not sufficient to fully train models that also require linguistic understanding.
elements.sent-2411-041: To overcome the aforementioned challenge, we utilize a four-stage training schema: pre-training vision encoder, pre-training VLM, instruction fine-tuning (IFT), and IFT with domain expert information.
elements.sent-2411-042: The two stages of IFT ensure that models can preserve their language capabilities without sacrificing performance on medical benchmarks \cite{hartsock2024vision .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This approach not only enhances their overall performance but also addresses the unique challenges posed by medical language and its precision requirements.
elements.sent-2411-044: Motivated by the above challenges, we present the following key contributions of our work:.
elements.sent-2411-045: Expert Knowledge Integration: We emphasize the need for integrating expert knowledge into medical VLMs to improve precision.
elements.sent-2411-046: Comprehensive capability: VILA-M3 is the first medical VLM that can tackle segmentation, classification, report generation, and VQA tasks in one framework.
elements.sent-2411-047: Expert-guided instruction fine-tuning: We preserve the VLMs language capabilities by introducing expert-guided IFT training on top of the built VLM training schemes.
elements.sent-2411-048: Hybrid 2D/3D Information Fusion: We introduce effective integration with 2D and 3D expert models, enabling hybrid fusion of domain expert models that provide relevant 3D spatial information to enhance VLMs limited to 2D inputs.
elements.sent-2411-049: Open-Source Module: We provide an open-source module for data preparation, training, and model evaluation in medical VLM.

elements.sent-2411-050: Vision-language models for medicine: VQA tasks were one of the first that combined text and images directly in a single task~\cite{antol2015vqa .}
elements.sent-2411-051: These were later extended to FLAN-style visual instruction tuning tasks~\cite{wei2021finetuned,xu2024vision .}
elements.sent-2411-052: General-purpose large multi-modal models such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt and Gemini~\cite{team2023gemini based on transformers~\cite{vaswani2017attention have demonstrated great potential towards building intelligent conversational assistants built on combined textual and vision datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: When applied in the biomedical domain, the models present promising capabilities of understanding the basic concepts.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, handling complex domain-specific tasks with high accuracy and fine granularity remains both challenging and demanding~\cite{yang2024advancing, li2024llava, saab2024capabilities, moor2023med .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Pre-training and instruction tuning is important for aligning the representations across modalities and enhancing the reasoning capabilities~\cite{lin2024vila, yang2024advancing .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Concurrently, there have been multiple methods targeting large VLMs for medicine in different aspects.
elements.sent-2411-057: Med-Gemini~\cite{yang2024advancing boasts a 1.5 Trillion parameter model and includes non-vison tasks like genomics and medical exams.}
elements.sent-2411-058: LLaVa-Med~\cite{li2024llava , based on the popular LLaVa architecture~\cite{liu2024visual , focused on using academic medical datasets, while Med-Flamingo \cite{moor2023med is an extension of Flamingo \cite{alayrac2022flamingo that introduced a novel mechanism for combining vision and text.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: BiomedParse allows promptable segmentations to be performed by the VLM itself~\cite{zhao2024biomedparse but does not generalize to more complex tasks like report generation.}
elements.sent-2411-060: While promising first steps towards VLMs in medicine, these works highlight the limitations in precision and lack of domain expertise when applied to medical tasks, compared to traditional computer vision applications on narrow tasks such as classification and segmentation, where traditional CNNs often outperform their VLM counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-061: Medical vision models and expert systems: Many models have been developed to address domain- and task-specific challenges.
elements.sent-2411-062: They achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performances in certain areas and often focus on expert knowledge learned from carefully curated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, Myronenko et al.~\cite{myronenko20193d created accurate lesion segmentation models for multi-modal brain MRI, He et al.~\cite{he2024vista3d studied 127 common types of human anatomical structures segmentation from tens of thousands of expert annotated CT examples.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Several chest X-ray classification models have been developed by Cohen et al.~\cite{Cohen2022xrv on a wide range of publicly available datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Integrating domain knowledge and guided reasoning while maintaining modular flexible system design can be a plausible approach to leverage the previous successes~\cite{wei2024mc,nisar2024d .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Evaluation and benchmarking in healthcare AI: Evaluation for vision-language models is a challenging task and is heavily dependent upon the type of task that is being evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-067: While it is straightforward to evaluate close-ended tasks such as classification, segmentation, or object detection as the well-established metrics can be directly used (e.g., F1, Dice, accuracy, etc.), it is not well-established how to evaluate more open-ended tasks where the answers have a wide range for variations.
elements.sent-2411-068: For example, open-ended questions in VQA and report generation require the evaluation of the correctness of entire sentences or paragraphs, which is challenging~\cite{ging2024open .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Many prior works attempted to evaluate the open-ended weaker VLM output by a stronger VLM.
elements.sent-2411-070: For instance, ChatGPT-4o is being used as a judge to evaluate the answer \cite{liu2024visual, li2024llava, wu2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-071: While using stronger VLMs as judges might be acceptable for computer vision tasks, this method of evaluation is not satisfactory for healthcare, where a factual level of precision is required, and patient outcomes depend on the results.
elements.sent-2411-072: Our evaluation shows that the capabilities of off-the-shelf VLMs like ChatGPT-4o are too limited to judge the outputs of medical VLMs directly.
elements.sent-2411-073: Agentic systems: The flexibility of generative AI provides new possibilities for developing powerful medical agents that can integrate heterogeneous information and generic reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-074: Most previous works focus on extending large language models in a medical context~\cite{tang2023medagents,yang2024drhouse,schmidgall2024agentclinic,li2024agent,wei2024medco .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Very recently, Hoopes et al.~\cite{hoopes2024voxelprompt developed a code-predicting agent framework that can integrate an array of external systems and APIs for a set of neuroimaging tasks including brain region analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Li et al.~\cite{li2024mmedagent proposed a multi-modal large language model invoking task-specific tools and aggregating results from them.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The VILA framework~\cite{lin2024vila is suitable for pre-training vision and language models as well as instruction fine-tuning (IFT) them with domain-specific datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-078: VILA-M3, in particular, leverages domain-expert model insights alongside the versatile capabilities of VILA to capture the nuances of radiological language and the intricate details of medical images, enabling more precise image-text fusion.
elements.sent-2411-079: It enhances tasks such as visual question answering (VQA), zero-shot image classification, image retrieval, and report generation, and it even incorporates expert model feedback to produce more accurate and well-reasoned outputs.
elements.sent-2411-080: VILA is particularly applicable for medical AI, where accurate association between visual data and textual information is critical for clinical decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-081: We further enhance it by including expert model feedback that can be triggered on demand.
elements.sent-2411-082: The standard approach to train VLMs like VILA is to use a corpus of visual-text data where text based conversations are associated with an image.
elements.sent-2411-083: The image token is randomly inserted in between the text or before/after the text (Fig.\ref{fig:radar_arch_plot ).}
elements.sent-2411-084: As a common practice that the inclusion of text-only data is helpful towards preserving the language capabilities of VLM, we included a small corpus of medical text data \cite{jin2021disease to specifically prevent degradation of the LLM capabilities as the model gets conditioned towards medical images.}
elements.sent-2411-085: VILA is based on auto-regressive multi-modal LLMs, where images are tokenized into visual tokens, concatenated with text tokens, and fed into language models, effectively treating visual input as a foreign language (Fig.\ref{fig:radar_arch_plot ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, a projector layer is needed to bridge the token embeddings from the image and text modalities.
elements.sent-2411-087: While different projector architectures are possible, a simple linear layer encourages the LLM to generalize better~\cite{lin2024vila .}
elements.sent-2411-088: This approach naturally extends text-only LLMs by incorporating visual embeddings, allowing them to handle interleaved image-text inputs and generate text outputs.
elements.sent-2411-089: There are other frameworks available that combine vision and language, such as cross-attention based \cite{moor2023med, alayrac2022flamingo , but auto-regressive approaches are gaining in popularity due to their flexibility in supporting different vision encoder and LLM backbones.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Therefore, we chose VILA as it is the current SOTA for traditional computer vision and is an open-source framework.
elements.sent-2411-091: VILA models are typically trained in three training stages: projector pre-training, visual language pre-training, and IFT.
elements.sent-2411-092: VILA-M3 builds on the last stage of visual instruction tuning and expands it with the inclusion of information from domain expert models.
elements.sent-2411-093: While VILA has enough flexibility to allow for separate fine-tuning of the vision encoder, the projector, and the LLM, we fine-tune all as it is necessary to customize both vision, projector alignment, and LLM for the medical domain as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:overview .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[trim={0 2em 0 0},clip,width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/fig_6_expert_qual_seg.pdf} \caption{Feedback of segmentation results can improve the quality of responses received from VLMs. This observation holds true for both VILA-M3 and GPT-4o. The models without expert segmentation fail to detect the tumor, unlike the models with access to expert model segmentation. The blue annotation box shows the marked tumor location, traditional VLM’s cannot capture such fine features unless guided by expert outcomes.} \label{fig:qual_expert_seg}
elements.sent-2411-094: In this work, we use open-source expert models for common medical imaging tasks.
elements.sent-2411-095: VILA-M3 learns to predict a keyword and argument, e.g., <VISTA3D(hepatic tumor)> when it wants to trigger a suitable expert model.
elements.sent-2411-096: The list of available expert models is fed as a system prompt to the model (see Fig.~\ref{fig:overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-097: Used Expert Models: For volumetric segmentation in CT images, we utilize VISTA3D ~\cite{he2024vista3d .}
elements.sent-2411-098: VISTA3D is a domain-specialized interactive foundation model developed for segmenting and annotating human anatomies with precision.
elements.sent-2411-099: It achieves a highly accurate segmentation of 127 classes, including challenging tumors.
elements.sent-2411-100: We include several organ or anatomy subgroups that can be chosen by VILA-M3 using different arguments when predicting the model keyword.
elements.sent-2411-101: Valid arguments are `everything' (i.e., all 127 available organ classes), `hepatic tumor', `pancreatic tumor', `lung tumor', etc. These arguments are described in model cards available to VILA-M3 as context at training and inference time (see Fig.~\ref{fig:radar_arch_plot ).}
elements.sent-2411-102: Note that when triggered, VISTA3D can segment the full volumetric scan even though VILA-M3 only processes 2D inputs.
elements.sent-2411-103: For MRI imaging, we focus on tumor segmentation in multimodal brain MRI.
elements.sent-2411-104: The MONAI BRATS model ~\cite{myronenko20193d consists of a pre-trained model for volumetric (3D) segmentation of brain tumor sub-regions from four MRI modalities (T1-weighted, T1-weighted with gadolinium contrast-enhancement, T2-weighted, and FLAIR) based on BraTS 2018 data and achieves good performance~\cite{menze2014multimodal .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The majority of the data sets used for training contain chest X-rays (CXRs), which are used for many routine diagnostic clinical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-106: In this work, we utilize a model ensemble of CNN-based classification models from TorchXRayVision ~\cite{Cohen2022xrv .}
elements.sent-2411-107: These models are trained on large cohorts of data and provide good accuracy across several diverse CXR datasets.
elements.sent-2411-108: Expert Model Feedback: When VILA-M3 selects and activates an appropriate expert model from the available model cards, the result is returned to VILA-M3 as another user prompt, formatted in a conversational style.
elements.sent-2411-109: For a segmentation result, we re-tokenize the generated mask and provide a textual description that explains the significance of the segmentation results, overlaid on the original image(see Fig.~\ref{fig:qual_expert_seg ).}
elements.sent-2411-110: In the case of classification, we fed back the result as a list of yes/no statements for the likelihood that the image contains a certain disease (i.e., a list of 18 diseases using the TorchXRayVision ensemble).
elements.sent-2411-111: Multiple publicly available datasets were utilized for this study.
elements.sent-2411-112: We present a brief overview of them and the processing that we applied to effectively utilize them for training with the VILA framework.
elements.sent-2411-113: All training data is detailed in Supp.. material.
elements.sent-2411-114: VQA: In this study, we utilize the RadVQA, SLAKE, and PathVQA datasets to evaluate visual question answering (VQA) performance in medical imaging \cite{he2020pathvqa, liu2021slake, lau2018dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-115: The SLAKE dataset comprises 642 medical images across modalities such as CXR, CT, and MRI, accompanied by 14,028 question-answer pairs covering radiology across various anatomical regions.
elements.sent-2411-116: From the official dataset splits, we used 4,919 training, 1,053 validation, and 1,061 test examples, focusing on diverse question formats like open-ended and yes/No. The PathVQA dataset includes 4,289 pathology images paired with 32,632 question-answer pairs, divided into 19,654 for training, 6,259 for validation, and 6,719 for testing.
elements.sent-2411-117: Both datasets probe a wide range of visual and clinical attributes, including abnormalities, organ identification, and diagnostic information.
elements.sent-2411-118: Our analysis focuses on using these datasets to enhance the performance of medical vision language models.
elements.sent-2411-119: We utilized both the training and validation sets from all the VQA datasets as part of the model training, due to the huge compute cost of training VLM's standard validation techniques are quite compute heavy and also redundant.
elements.sent-2411-120: Classification: We adopted two publicly available high-quality datasets of abnormalities in CXR images as a benchmark for the model's visual classification capability.
elements.sent-2411-121: One is the subset of {ChestX-ray14 ~\cite{wang2017chestxray curated by Majkowska and Mittal et al.~\cite{doi:10.1148/radiol.2019191293 , consisted of 1,962 images after an adjudication process of four radiologists.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: For this dataset, we follow~\cite{yang2024advancing and focus on the classification of three conditions -- lung opacity, pneumothorax, and fracture.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The other dataset is a subset of {CheXpert ~\cite{irvin2019chexpert created by Saporta et al.~\cite{saporta2022benchmarking on the labeling of five pathological conditions, namely atelectasis, cardiomegaly, consolidation, edema, and pleural effusion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Report Generation: For report generation, we adopt the MIMIC-CXR\footnote{v2.1.0 dataset~\cite{johnson2019mimiccxr,johnson2019mimic1cxrjpg , which provides over 377,110 CXR images in JPG format, along with structured labels extracted from 227,827 associated free-text radiology reports.}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Each image is linked to the corresponding radiology report, which contains the radiologists' findings and impressions.
elements.sent-2411-126: Labels for the dataset were derived using automated tools CheXpert~\cite{irvin2019chexpert and NegBio~\cite{peng2018negbio , which extract and classify medical conditions (e.g., ``pneumonia,'' ``edema,'' ``pleural effusion'') as positive, negative, uncertain, or not mentioned based on the language of the reports.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Additionally, radiologists manually annotate a portion of the report, providing 14 categories of findings for model evaluation and validation.
elements.sent-2411-128: The dataset is divided into training, validation, and testing parts, each with separate CSV files to support reproducible research.
elements.sent-2411-129: Due to suboptimal report quality in the initial datasets, we applied advanced Large Language Models (LLMs) to refine text reports through targeted noise reduction and finding extraction.
elements.sent-2411-130: This process significantly improved model accuracy and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-131: Additional methodological details are available in the supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-132: Expert Datasets: To create training data that triggers expert models for tasks like segmentation and classification, we leverage existing datasets by running expert model inference.
elements.sent-2411-133: For instance, we can segment existing CT datasets with the VISTA3D model, using the inference results to build training conversations tailored for VILA-M3.
elements.sent-2411-134: Similarly, with a brain MRI dataset, we can run the BRATS segmentation model and then utilize the output to generate structured training conversations for training VILA-M3.
elements.sent-2411-135: This approach facilitates the generation of context-specific dialogues that enhance model alignment with expert-level tasks for medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-136: Further, we extend a subset of the existing VQA and report generation tasks by adding the model cards to the message context and adding trigger statements for the corresponding CXR expert model as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:radar_arch_plot .}
elements.sent-2411-137: We use the image resolution in the visual encoder at 384$\times$384 using OpenAI's CLIP-L, enabling the model to capture more fine-grained visual details.
elements.sent-2411-138: For the LLM backbones, we use Vicuna (based on Llama-2 \cite{touvron2023llama ) for 3 Billion and 13 Billion parameter checkpoints, for 8 Billion, we utilize Llama-3 \cite{dubey2024llama as the backbone and the 40 Billion uses Yi-34B as the LLM backbone based on Yi \cite{young2024yi and uses a 448$\times$448 vision encoder.}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The LLM choices depend on the configurations used for the VILA checkpoints pre-trained on natural images.
elements.sent-2411-140: Training Hyper-parameters: To ensure a fair comparison for all VILA model size variants, we kept the same training hyper-parameters for each size.
elements.sent-2411-141: A consistent batch size of 16 was maintained at the device (per GPU) level for training and 4 for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-142: Gradient accumulation was adjusted accordingly to ensure the same overall effective batch size in multi-node training.
elements.sent-2411-143: The learning rate was initialized at 2e-5, using a cosine learning rate schedule with a warm-up ratio of 0.03.
elements.sent-2411-144: Weight decay was set to zero to avoid over-regularization, and all the models utilized bf16 mixed precision, allowing efficient training with a reduced memory footprint.
elements.sent-2411-145: Gradient check-pointing was enabled to further optimize memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-146: The LLM backbone, projector, and vision encoder were kept unfrozen as that is the best setting to adapt for new data as per \cite{lin2024vila to fine-tune the entire model.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Training Cost: To train these models we used A100's with 80GB of memory.
elements.sent-2411-148: For the 3, 8, and 13 billion parameter models, we utilized four nodes (x8 A100) in parallel using 32 GPUs in total.
elements.sent-2411-149: For the 40B model, we utilized sixteen nodes, utilizing a total of 128 GPUs in parallel (Table~\ref{tab:training_compute_cost ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: More information regarding inference computational cost can be found in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-151: Inference Cost: With the open source release\footnote{github url~\url{[BLINDED] the computational footprints are as follows: The CXR expert dynamically loads various TorchXrayVision models and performs ensemble predictions.}}

elements.sent-2411-189: The proposed VILA-M3 model represents a significant advancement in multimodal machine learning for healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-190: It achieves SOTA performance as both a generalist model and one incorporating expert model responses to solve complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-191: This capability allows the model to maintain high levels of performance when addressing general tasks while also leveraging expert knowledge to improve accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-192: With additional expert models, it can be seamlessly extended to various capabilities, such as registration, regression, etc. Our work can be seen as a step towards enabling ``chain of thought'' capabilities in VLMs by letting the model reason about when to trigger an expert and how to incorporate its result in the final generated prediction.
elements.sent-2411-193: For future work, we will explore the integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to further enhance VILA-M3 by retrieving and incorporating relevant information from large datasets dynamically during inference.
elements.sent-2411-194: Additionally, we aim to expand VILA-M3 towards a multi-agent framework that directly incorporates smart expert models that can themselves decide whether to involve additional experts for consultation~\cite{guo2024large to optimize performance across varied tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-195: In conclusion, the proposed VILA-M3 framework can be used to train SOTA models that consider expert model responses to improve performance on particular tasks while simultaneously exhibiting good generalist performance.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Abstract: Generalist vision language models (VLMs) have made significant strides in computer vision, but they fall short in specialized fields like healthcare, where expert knowledge is essential.
elements.sent-2411-197: Domain expert models developed for medical use are crucial because they are specifically trained for certain clinical tasks, e.g. to detect tumors and classify abnormalities through segmentation and classification, which learn fine-grained features of medical data$-$features that are often too intricate for a VLM to capture effectively.

elements.sent-2411-205: The preparation involves downloading datasets and refining report text with a Large Language Model (LLM) to create high-quality inputs for generating reliable medical reports.
elements.sent-2411-206: The primary dataset used is the MIMIC Chest X-ray JPG Database v2.0.0 ~\cite{johnson2019mimiccxr,johnson2019mimic1cxrjpg , which contains over 377,000 images and 227,827 free-text radiology reports.}
elements.sent-2411-207: These data are de-identified to comply with HIPAA requirements.
elements.sent-2411-208: The process incorporates text enhancements and cleansing to optimize the quality of report inputs.
elements.sent-2411-209: The process begins with downloading the MIMIC-CXR-JPG dataset, which provides chest X-ray images and corresponding radiology reports.
elements.sent-2411-210: Data splits and labels are standardized, and enhanced text versions are utilized for improved report quality.
elements.sent-2411-211: The dataset is designed for tasks involving medical image analysis and natural language processing.
elements.sent-2411-212: Enhanced text versions ensure clarity and remove noise for better performance in model training.
elements.sent-2411-213: To refine the quality of the reports and eliminate noise, we utilize an enhanced text version developed by DCL~\cite{li2023dynamic , and subsequently apply additional cleansing procedures to further optimize report accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-214: To standardize the language used in medical reports, a pool of sample sentences is created.
elements.sent-2411-215: This pool consists of commonly recurring phrases or sentence structures that appear in radiology reports.
elements.sent-2411-216: Using python scripts, the LLM (Llama-3.1-8b-instruct) analyzes the dataset and extracts these patterns, which are stored in a file (e.g., sentence-pool.txt) to guide the text transformation process.
elements.sent-2411-217: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size.
elements.sent-2411-218: The lungs are low in volume.
elements.sent-2411-221: There is mild pulmonary vascular congestion.
elements.sent-2411-222: Prompt Example for Collection: "Analyze the medical report dataset and extract recurring sentence structures.
elements.sent-2411-223: Compile these into a pool of sample sentences to be used for standardizing input text.".
elements.sent-2411-224: The LLM (Llama-3.1-8b-instruct) utilizes the sentence pool to process the text, replacing or reformatting sentences and medical terminology into a consistent and standardized format.
elements.sent-2411-225: This step ensures uniform input data for the VLM model, improving its ability to generate reliable and accurate reports.
elements.sent-2411-226: Before Conversion: "Lungs are low in volume.
elements.sent-2411-227: Congestion of the pulmonary vasculature, small bilateral pleural effusions and presence of septal lines reflect mild pulmonary edema.".
elements.sent-2411-228: After Conversion: "The lungs are low in volume.
elements.sent-2411-230: Prompt Example for Conversion: "Using the provided sentence pool, transform the medical report text into a consistent format.
elements.sent-2411-231: Ensure the output text aligns with the predefined sentence structures without altering the content's meaning.
elements.sent-2411-233: Segmentation: For segmentation models, we can train VILA-M3 to trigger a suitable expert model together with several different input arguments.
elements.sent-2411-234: For example, when triggering the VISTA3D model, we generate several variations (using Llama-3.1) of instructions to the model to segment a certain anatomical structure in the image and give a suitable trigger message.. For instance:.
elements.sent-2411-235: User: Can you identify any liver masses or tumors?.
elements.sent-2411-236: VILA-M3: This looks like a CT image.
elements.sent-2411-238: User: Can you assist me in segmenting the bony structures in this image?.
elements.sent-2411-239: VILA-M3: I segmented the skeleton using <VISTA3D(skeleton)>.
elements.sent-2411-241: VILA-M3: I segmented the entire image using <VISTA3D(everything)>.
elements.sent-2411-242: For these types of instructions, VILA-M3 predicts the suitable arguments for the VISTA3D model in order to trigger the segmentation of the correct anatomical structure as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:segment_args .}
elements.sent-2411-243: We built these types of instruction and answer pairs into the expert training dataset in order to build segmentation capabilities into VILA-M3.

elements.sent-2411-245: Report Generation: Converting expert model predictions into conversation format involves transforming classification outputs into structured dialogues for AI training in medical report generation.
elements.sent-2411-246: Initially, ensemble predictions are created by combining probabilities of various medical conditions (like Atelectasis and Effusion) from multiple expert models from TorchXRayVision applied to chest X-ray images.
elements.sent-2411-247: These probabilities are interpreted to determine the presence (\textquotedblleft yes\textquotedblright) or absence (\textquotedblleft no\textquotedblright) of each condition based on a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-248: The formatted expert predictions are then integrated into conversation prompts that include an image placeholder, a prompt for report generation, and the expert results as additional information.
elements.sent-2411-249: For example, the prompt might be:.
elements.sent-2411-250: When answering, please incorporate the expert model results: Atelectasis: yes Cardiomegaly: no Effusion: yes.
elements.sent-2411-251: Each conversation is assembled into a structured format containing a unique identifier, the image reference, and the dialogue between the human and the VLM model.
elements.sent-2411-252: This approach enriches the dataset with various prompt types and simulates interactions where the VLM model provides diagnoses based on image analysis and expert insights, enhancing the model's ability to generate accurate and contextually rich medical reports.

elements.sent-2411-277: We believe the guardrails of GPT-4o likely come into effect when a sufficient amount of medical terminology is used within the prompt itself.

elements.sent-2411-285: We conducted a detailed analysis of the test set results for report generation, focusing on the disease and pattern distribution (multi-label ground truth) from the MIMIC Chest X-ray JPG Database v2.0.0.
elements.sent-2411-286: This evaluation utilized the best-performing 40B model, covering both with and without the integration of expert model predictions.
elements.sent-2411-287: Table~\ref{fig:detailed-report shows performance metrics with and without expert model predictions across various categories of medical findings.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Key metrics include BLEU-4, ROUGE, and GREEN score (GREEN), evaluated for both settings.
elements.sent-2411-289: For most categories, the inclusion of expert models resulted in marginal improvements in the GREEN metric, which measures overall accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-290: For instance, in the Atelectasis category, GREEN improved from 35.93 to 36.56, and in Cardiomegaly, it increased from 38.99 to 39.79.
elements.sent-2411-291: However, some metrics, such as BLEU-4 in categories like Fracture and Lung Lesion, showed slight decreases when expert model predictions were used.
elements.sent-2411-292: Notably, the No Finding category exhibited the largest improvement in green (45.60 to 46.57), indicating that expert models may be particularly effective in identifying cases with no abnormalities.
elements.sent-2411-293: These results suggest that while the integration of expert models generally enhances certain metrics, the gains are context-dependent and may vary across different medical conditions.

elements.sent-2411-294: As also stated in the main manuscript, we will explore the integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to further enhance VILA-M3 by retrieving and incorporating relevant information from large datasets dynamically during inference.
elements.sent-2411-295: Further improvements would be to make an agentic framework with expert models, however it requires careful design.
elements.sent-2411-296: However, implementing an agentic framework necessitates careful design to balance autonomy with control, ensuring the system remains reliable and interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-297: Addressing these design challenges will be crucial for advancing the model's capabilities and for its successful deployment in complex real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-298: The results of this work indicate that we need to carefully curate data and factor in expert information from the many medical domain-specialized models that have been trained in the past.
